macro_segments.title: The Givonim (Gibeonites): David's Edict Against Intermarriage

micro_segments.title: Introduction to Netinim and the Givonim as one of the Seven Nations
Prohibition of intermarriage: Lo titchaten bam and its application to converts
Rabbi Chana bar Adda: David's edict on the Netinim
The famine in David's time and investigation of causes (idolatry, sexual sin, broken pledges)
David consults God via Urim and Thummim; God identifies Saul's massacre of Givonim as cause
Why Saul's killing of Givonim is inferred from destruction of Nov and failure to sustain them
Punishment for both Saul's mistreatment and failure to eulogize him properly
David summons Givonim; they demand seven of Saul's sons as retribution
Jewish people's three characteristics: compassion, shame, and kindness
David's conclusion: Givonim unfit for intermarriage due to their cruelty

macro_segments.title: The Execution of Saul's Sons and Ritzpah's Vigil: Kiddush Hashem and National Character

micro_segments.title: Selection of seven sons via the Ark; Mephiboshes exempted through David's prayer
Violation of Torah law against punishing children for fathers' sins justified by Kiddush Hashem
Ritzpah's vigil over the bodies; the prohibition against leaving bodies unburied
Kiddush Hashem through demonstrating high ethical standards to the nations
150,000 converts join Israel in response; involvement in Temple construction

macro_segments.title: Status of Netinim: Servitude and Intermarriage Prohibitions from Yehoshua through Rabban Gamliel

micro_segments.title: Yehoshua's designation of Givonim as hewers of wood and drawers of water for the Altar
Moshe's edict for that generation; Yehoshua's edict contingent on Temple service
David's extension of the prohibition to future generations beyond Temple times
Attempted repeal in the time of Rabbi; Rabban Gamliel's position on Temple servitude
Distinction between people's share and Temple's share in Netinim status

macro_segments.title: Saris and Ailonit: Categories Excluded from Yibum and Chalitzah

micro_segments.title: Conflicting traditions about whether a saris can perform chalitzah
Rabbi Akiva's distinction: saris adam (human-caused) vs. saris chamah (natural)
Saris adam has sha'at kosher (prior capacity); saris chamah does not
Rabbi Eliezer's alternative: natural conditions potentially curable vs. permanent human damage
Testimony of Rabbi Yehoshua ben Betira regarding Ben Magusas: yibum performed despite saris adam status
Simple law: saris does not perform chalitzah or yibum; ailonit similarly excluded
Meaningless chalitzah by saris; sex becomes arayot, invalidating the woman
Same rules apply to ailonit regarding chalitzah and yibum

macro_segments.title: Gemara Analysis: Saris and the Problem of Prohibition Despite Obligation

micro_segments.title: Contradiction: saris adam is prohibited (lav), yet can perform yibum and chalitzah
Rabbi Ammi's resolution: we discuss a case of marrying a convert, where no prohibition applies
Rabbi Akiva holds like Rabbi Yossi: kahal gerim not considered kahal; prohibition inapplicable
Question: if no prohibition, why not also yibum, not just chalitzah?
Resolution: Rabbi Yehoshua would restrict even converts; Rabbi Akiva does not
Inference from testimony: yibum actually performed, supporting yibum possibility for saris adam

macro_segments.title: Saris as Dead Husband or Living Brother: Normalcy of Yibum and Chalitzah Obligations

micro_segments.title: If saris (dead or alive) or elderly person: chalitzah or yibum permissible and binding
Legal acts (maamar, get, chalitzah) and yibum all valid regardless of saris status
Prohibition remains: they cannot stay married due to lo yavo ptzua daka

macro_segments.title: The Problem of Later-Occurring Defects: Do They Undermine Yibum Obligations?

micro_segments.title: Rava's question: if saris status occurs after death of husband, does it negate yibum obligation?
Rava's proposal: defect occurring post-death does not retroactively affect yibum status
Abaye's objection: even later-occurring prohibition (achot isha) overrides yibum obligation
Case of underage wife: if she matures, wife's sister status cancels yibum obligation
Conclusion: later-occurring achot isha prohibition cancels yibum, so too should ptzua daka

macro_segments.title: Resolution: Pftzua Daka as a Simple Lav, Not Arayot-Related Lav

micro_segments.title: Rav Yosef's answer: Rabbi Akiva distinguishes between arayot-related lavim and simple lavim
Ptzua daka is a simple lav unrelated to kinship; not treated as issur karet by Rabbi Akiva
Only kinship-related lavim (chayavei lavin d'she'er) produce mamzer status and exclude yibum

topical_tags.term: Yehoshua's designation of hewers of wood
Chayavei lavin (those obligated by negative commands)
Rav Yosef's distinction of lav types
